speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Minia Jay. We were talking about your father and you were saying that you remember the big books. Can you tell me what you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Every Shabbat, he would always read a portion from the books. And this was for him-- this was his life. He was sincerely a religious man. And he believed very much. He was a tall man. And he was six foot tall, blond, blue eyes. Good-looking man. And he liked to dress well. Yeah. Always with a smile.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he wearing, normally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, normally, he would wear you know, the clothes which Chasidim were wearing, right? But he would wear also a suit. Shabbas he would wear the Shabbas thing. But in winter, he was wearing always a very nice fur coat with a nice [INAUDIBLE], you know, that they had, because Poland is a cold country. And he looked good. Yeah. He was a very kind man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of relationship did you have to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my relationship was much, much closer with my mother. I was a really mother's child. My father, yes, he always, when I needed something, he used to take me. He even used to go shopping with me to buy clothes for me, so on. He was a very kind man. But the relationship with my mother was different. Also, I was a young girl, and even when I was a child, my mother talked to me like to a grownup. I felt so good when I was with my mother. And, of course, when, in the end, they took my mother away, it was terrible. Terrible. Unbelievable. Can't really describe it. And I can tell you one thing. I could never talk about it before. When I was a young girl and I came to England, straight after the war, I could never talk about my family. It was too hurting every time. I even to my children-- I never told my children anything at the time, because it was very painful. Very painful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a bit about your mother? What was she doing in the house? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was always seeing that everything should run smoothly, everybody should be all right. There was always-- she was always in to see that the family is OK and always food on the table and everything. And the Yom Tov prepared, and Shabbat is-- She was busy with family. She had a big family. But she was a very happy person. She was a very good, good [INAUDIBLE], very happy person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew my grandparents. I was the youngest, and none of my grandparents were alive. In fact, my name is after my grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandmother's name was Mendel Minia, and this is what I am. My Jewish name is Mendel. Mendel. Minda, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what your parents talked about in the house? What was talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the parents-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] look, in front of the children, they didn't talk so much. Whatever they had to say and so on, it was always between them, but not in front of the children. Probably because they didn't want us to know any problems or whatever. We only-- whenever they were with us, they showed, you know, that they were happy. They were OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many hours of the day would you spend in the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went first to the Polish school in the morning. And then I would come home. One o'clock it was finished, the school, so I came home for lunch. And then I would go back to the Jewish school about half past two to the Jewish school. And I stayed there about a couple of hours in the Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I liked very much going there, because it was nice being with all the children. We knew each other. We were happy. We were singing songs together. And so it was nice. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where you were when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was at home. And we heard on the radio that the war is out, is broken, that is, the war just started. It was such a shock, because we never believed it. But as children, we didn't see anything happening yet. We only heard it on the radio. And then suddenly there was so much chaos going on. And it was terrible. And straightaway there was-- they came and they took our horses away, you know. But they left us one horse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took away the horses?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles. They needed it for the-- Poland didn't have so many machinery. They didn't have so many cars. And they needed the horses for transporting and so on. And soon they were talking about evacuation. We would have to leave the town. I had my sister. One married sister came for summer with her two children. And these children never wanted to eat. My sister always complained they didn't want to eat. And that was really something I shall never forget. When the war broke out, nobody had time, and everybody was so worried. And suddenly there was a piece of old bread lying on the table. And the little one who could hardly reach the table, he tried to get that piece of old bread, which normally he wouldn't even eat anything. We always-- she had to run after them to feed them, my sister. But, anyway, we were evacuated all together. We went-- we had a cart and had a horse. Most people had to go walking and on bicycles, whatever they had, what means. So we went to Lodz and stayed there a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother had the best idea. She was a really clever woman. She said, why don't we leave it all and go into Russia? And from Russia, maybe we could somehow get to Palestine or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as soon as we hear there was the clear given that we can go back to our town, my father wouldn't hear of it. He said, oh, no. Because from generations they lived in this town. And we went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, and the first thing when we passed the bridge and we saw what this very big building, which was for hay, you know? Was a wooden, big, enormous-- like a shed, but enormous one, built from wood for hay and straw. I don't know. My father said, ah, everything's intact. We came home. Our house was intact. And in actual fact, it would have been better if everything would have been burned down. Then we probably-- they would have probably survived. They would have probably never stayed there, you see? So when did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we had arrived, everything was intact. Except in the center of the town, a lot of houses were burned down, because when the Germans arrived, the first thing what they did, they threw some bombs down everywhere. So what had hit actually was the center, half of the area from the houses. And our synagogue was burned down. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else went with you to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was my sister and the two children. My father, and me, and the two other sisters, not married ones. My oldest brother was taken. He was in Pabianice, anyway. He lived in Pabianice, but they've taken him to very hard working-- they've taken him to a labor camp where he had to work on the railways. It was the hardest work you could have, with no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as the war broke out. Soon after they taking him. And my other brother, they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, in the street. They called him. I don't know whether he didn't-- he was distracted and didn't hear them. So they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got to know it because somebody came to tell us that Welwel has been shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war actually broke out, do you remember, did you hear anything about Hitler or about Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, yes. That, yes we did. Actually, already at school, some of the kids would say to you, wait till Hitler comes. Wait till Hitler comes. They would-- they had it from their parents. And the Poles were very anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you reply?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I reply? We often didn't. What could we say to them? Nothing. What could you reply to children that say wait till-- sometimes you did say something. But nothing-- what could you do? It wasn't the right thing to say to you, anyway. But in fact, when the war broke out and I mean, the Germans marched in, some of the Poles didn't help us. On the contrary, when we were in the street or whatever, and the Germans needed people, they'd say, [POLISH]. We are Jews. And on one of these occasions, they caught me and my friends. We were going-- was a Shabbat day. We were going for a walk. And suddenly, as we came back, Germans and Polish boys said, [POLISH]. Jews. And they were chasing us to the center of the town to clear out rubble from the burned houses. We were only kids, really, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Which month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the beginning of the war. Right at the beginning of the war, because they wanted these houses straightaway cleared. The German straightaway got themself busy when they got to-- when they occupied the country. And who should they get? The Jews, to clear out the rubble and do all the dirty work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in this occasion, they lined us up. And a man was in the cellar, and he was throwing out the bricks. And one girl was in front of me. I was right behind her. We were in a line. The last one would put it on a heap. And this girl, she couldn't catch not one brick. So every brick fell. And when they fell, they fell on my ankle, on my foot. My foot started bleeding. It was terrible. At a certain moment, they said the buergermeister, which was the German magistrate, who was from Germany, actually sent from the Third Reich, sent to our town, and they were throwing the stones even quicker. At that moment, I could see another brick fall on my foot. I thought, this girl is going to kill me. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, and her name was Golda. I said, Golda, move up. You stay behind me. And I was standing in front. And I was catching every stone and giving it to her into her hand, sort of thing. And as it happens, the burgermeister was already standing in front of us. And he saw us. He called me out. He said, [GERMAN]? What is your name? And I said Minia. Well, of course, in German, Mina. So he said, Mina. And then Munter is a German name. He said, Mina Munter.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- couldn't believe that I was Jewish. I looked white. I was blonde. And I was a tall, slim girl. For my age I was quite tall. Anyway, he said [GERMAN]. You are a good worker. And this remained. It was a German in those, days during the war, to be called a good worker, you already-- that was very important. And every time, whatever happened later on, I was always-- I was favored by him, that I'm a good worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you continue going to school in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The school closed down straightaway when we came back. As soon as-- look. The war broke out in September or end of August, September. And there was no school from that onwards at all. On the contrary, the school they brought some Germans, I don't know from which part of the countries, whether they were from Hungary or from somewhere. They looked like gypsies. But they were also Germans. And they were put into these schools. There were hospitals before the war in our area, in Warta. They emptied all the people out, because they send them to be gassed, and put in their own people. Whenever they needed people for work, they would come out with big sticks. And God forbid if you got hit by them. Then you could also not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how long did you spend away before you came back to Warta?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you-- were you away from Warta when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in Warta all the time, until 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but you said immediately when the war broke out, that your father and you and your mother you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were evacuated. But we came back one week. Just one week. After that week, we came back to Warta, to the town. And we were there till 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe me what were you doing in that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, In Warta, later on we always had to work, because if we didn't, we were always afraid that if somebody doesn't work, they would send you straightaway somewhere. Because people who didn't work were no good to the Germans. So later on-- first, they were getting us to do this work, this clearing. And then, afterwards, they opened up some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tailoring places. What they did is one part of the town, in the center, on the first floor, they opened up-- each room was opened up to make one area. And there were four tailors from our town. Three male tailors, and one was a ladies' tailor, but he was also a man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were making to measure the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call-- for the Germans, for the Gestapo? The uniforms. The German uniforms, because they had it done specially to measure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on one of these occasions-- when we heard that-- when they opened this up, my mother said-- she spoke to one of the tailors and asked him to take me along. I should learn do something. Give her something to do. She must do something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how I started sewing on buttons, doing other things. But I was working. And the burgermeister came up with the Gestapo. And he always noticed me. He would say she is the best [GERMAN], you know? The best tailor. I-- [INAUDIBLE] an idea, but they like-- but I had a good-- whatever I did was already good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is what happened. We were there till 1942. And then that morning they rounded us all up. Four o'clock in the morning, we all had to get up. Get out. We were all asleep. We all had to get out of bed, and they rounded us up. And every family was standing together. And they only wanted to take out a few people from this town, because they didn't want any of the people. So of course the ill, the old, and the children, and young people. Many young people, because they didn't want all these young people. They actually only wanted people who had a trade, a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] artisan. And so many of our people were just sent to one church. And as it happens, when the alteste der Juden, who was in charge--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alteste der Juden. That means the oldest, the elder. The elder of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember his name, no. He and-- I could find out. That I would find all right-- he and the burgermeister and the Gestapo, they all were walking around. And the eldest of the Juden took some people out. But he couldn't take everybody out. And as it happens, the burgermeister took me out. He said I am good dressmaker, because only artisans could come out. As a worker, you couldn't this time come out. Only as a artisan. And this is how I came out. That was the first opportunity to get out of the town. Otherwise, I would have not come out in the first place. I wouldn't even be able to talk about anything, because I would be sent with my parents. But I was the first one to be taken out from my family. And I didn't want to go, because I wanted not to leave my mother. And my mother kept saying to me, go. Go. She made me. She pushed me, in fact. She said, go. Go. It was a terrible time, that. When I left [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left my mother-- because even afterwards, she said, my youngest child, how is she going to cope? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Was terrible. How did you leave? Did you leave by train, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were sent to a different church at first. And by lunch time, around, I don't know, one o'clock or 12 o'clock, my sister arrived. She was taken out as well. And my sister and myself, we went [INAUDIBLE] up a lorry to the ghetto, to Lodz ghetto. When I arrived in the Lodz ghetto, I never believed what I saw. They sent us to a prison, or near the prison. Not into the prison, but near the prison, where children were imprisoned for taking a potato or-- to survive. And when I saw these children, I said, God forbid, I'll never look like that. But after two years I got very, very ill. Very ill there. I was so thin you can't imagine when you would look at me now. Yeah. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about the Lodz ghetto, can we maybe just go back to that period until '42? What was the food situation like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in a nice part of the town, which was going down near the river. And it was nice. And we had to move to the other side, to the poorer side of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I think in 1940. Sometime in 1940, we had to move to that area. Naturally, some of the things we couldn't carry-- have furniture, like, and other things, because this was smaller accommodation. Only a couple of rooms, and very poor quality, you know? And we had to leave the things in our house. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very difficult, because in the ghetto, you couldn't-- we had no chemists in the ghetto. Everything was outside. You weren't allowed to go outside the ghetto. But so happens that I did go outside the ghetto. First of all, I was a very courageous girl. But also the thing was that around 1940, I think it was, beginning-- or was it end of 1939-- the eldest of the Juden, and the elder, came to my mother and said, would my mother let me go and work for the meister, which was the top of the gendarmerie, which was the police, the German police. He was the top of them. And they came from Germany, and they had a little girl. She was about two years, or two and a half years old. And they were only a family with this one child. And he asked me that-- well, apparently, she wanted someone to look after the child, to go for a walk with them more than anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother wouldn't allow it. My mother said no, none of my children are going to work for anybody and any Germans. And just leave us alone, and don't come to our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had the feeling that I wanted to do it. It's really like fate. I ran after him, and I said, I want to go. And I will go. And that helped us so much. First of all, because when I went upstairs, and I came upstairs, and I was wearing a scarf. I put a big scarf on, which my mother once made, like a crochet scarf, different colors it had. And I came up. And I had my stars of David, one front and one in the back. When I came upstairs, and she said I should take the scarf off, and she saw that. She said, this you will not wear in my place. So straightaway, I had to take these off. And then I could go everywhere for a walk with the little girl. And I didn't wear the star. So I could do what I wanted. Everybody who wanted something-- sometimes they had a headache or toothache, or this or that-- I was always running to the chemists, because I could go, you see. And what's more, when I went past the where the magistrate is, because the chemist was behind there, and the burgermeister had his office. And do you know, the window was always open summertime. Wide open.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would wave to him and say hello, he would wave right back. I didn't know what it was, whether I reminded him of-- because he was from Germany, but his family was in Germany-- whether I-- Munter. He thought I was-- I am a German girl, you know? That my family come from Germans or something. Because he once said, [GERMAN]? He said, how comes that your name is Munter? Somebody's German in your family, which is-- I don't know about at all. So I used to always run about, bring everybody something, and the chemist, and whatever. If we wanted something, I could go out. And I wasn't afraid to go, for a start, because she said I shouldn't wear the stars. So whatever, whoever would have stopped me-- but nobody actually stopped me, because I didn't look Jewish, you know. In a way it was-- so. What was the name of the family? The German family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. I only know that he was the meister of the gendarmerie. Meister. He was the top. There was three top gendarmerie. One was a [GERMAN]. One was-- but he was the meister, the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember talking to his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we didn't talk very much. No. Just with the child I went for a walk, and so on. But I had always the radio going up there, because nobody was allowed to have a radio. We didn't have-- god forbid if somebody had a radio. And there I could hear [GERMAN]. They were always singing they're going against England. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was making me sick when I heard all these songs which they were singing, all their German songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when were you made to wear the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think end of '39. Yeah. The end of around, I don't know, October. Straightaway they wanted us to wear the stars. I think now first we were wearing a thing around, the band. And then we had to wear the stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, tell me, the ghetto. Was it cut off? How was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was-- we were all living now to one area, which was a cheaper area, where the poorer people lived. But it was not a closed ghetto. Not like in Lodz ghetto or any other. It just had a-- at the end of the road, it had a thing like that on poles. I don't know what this is called. A pole. Vertical pole, just-- you could only go so far.

speaker: interviewee
text: In any case, there were no cars there during the war. Who had, in fact, a car? I think somebody, only one person had a car. But you could only-- you were only allowed to go so far and not further.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, one boy, he was 13 years old, and he went outside the ghetto. Somebody recognized him, and they put him to prison and they hanged him. And the whole town had to come and see it. Everybody had to come to see the hanging of this boy. The boy was crying. He was frightened like anything. Oh, the things they did. And of course they also hanged the rabbi. The rav, our rav. And his brother-- his son. And the prominent Jews in the town were hanged. We all had to go and see it. Terrible things. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about that? Where were they hanged?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center of the town. They've got the-- like a square. Every little town has a square. From there all the roads come out of the square. And in the square they made a whole thing out of it. They made like a pantomime out of it. And they hanged the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what did they do? They picked up a Jew to hang a Jew. To afterwards, whatever it is, to show the world that the Jews are hanging the Jews. They picked out this one had to hang this one, and this one had to hang that one. Sorry, we have to change tapes.
